title: Pick the figure kind by the question the slide answers
body: Choose kind from intent: time order → timeline; a few headline numbers → kpi; compare N options across attributes → table (or compare for 2-3 rich panels); position on two axes → matrix; numeric values across categories → chart; part-of hierarchy → tree; process with arrows → flow; physical layer build-up → stack; lateral optical path → photonic. If two fit, prefer the one that needs the least fabricated data.

title: Chart vs KPI vs table
body: KPI: 2-6 single headline numbers, no series. Chart: the SAME metric across an ordered/compared axis (needs ≥3 real values). Table: several attributes × several options (a grid of mostly text). If you have one number per item, it is KPI, not a chart. If you have no real numbers, never draw a chart — use kpi/table with qualitative cells.

title: Chart honesty
body: Start bars at a zero baseline; do not truncate to exaggerate differences. Label the y-axis with its unit. Keep to ≤3 series (more becomes unreadable). Bars compare discrete categories; lines show a trend over an ordered axis. Never invent values — if the user gave none, ask or switch to a qualitative kind.

title: Table data-ink
body: Terse cells (a value or short phrase, not a sentence). First column is the row-label header. Emphasize at most ONE column (the recommended/our option) with a tint — not every column. Keep to ≤6 columns and ≤8 rows; if larger, split or summarize. Align consistently; let whitespace, not heavy borders, separate rows.

title: KPI card hierarchy
body: The value is the hero — largest type, with its unit ('1.2 TB/s', '99.2%'). The label is secondary and small. Show a delta only when there is a real baseline, and color it by meaning (good/bad), not decoration. 2-6 cards; beyond that a table serves better. Group related cards in one row.

title: Timeline density and emphasis
body: 3-8 milestones; more belongs in a table. Alternate labels above/below the axis to avoid collision. date_label is a display string ('2026 Q3') — never fabricate exact dates. Emphasize only 1-2 pivotal milestones. Use phases (spanning bars) for overlapping workstreams, not one bar per milestone.

title: 2x2 matrix axis naming
body: Name BOTH ends of each axis (low↔high) so the quadrants are interpretable. Keep ≤4 terse items per quadrant. Standard reading: top-right = high/high (usually 'act now'), bottom-left = low/low. Use a 2x2 for positioning/prioritization, not for a ranked list (use a table) or time order (timeline).

title: Hierarchy width and depth
body: Exactly one root. Keep depth ≤3 and ≤5 children per node — deeper/wider trees overflow a single figure; summarize or split. Each node id unique; every parent references an existing id. Use a tree for part-of/reports-to structure, not for a sequence with arrows (that is flow).

title: Semantic color, not decoration
body: In general infographics use semantic roles, never raw colors. accent1..6 DISTINGUISH categories (no inherent meaning); good/bad/warn/neutral carry MEANING (status, delta sign). Do not use good/bad decoratively. One highlight color per figure carries the 'so what'; everything else stays neutral. The engine maps roles to a consistent palette.

title: Number formatting
body: Use SI units and consistent significant figures (usually 2-3). Keep units with the value ('12 W', '48 h'). Do not over-precision ('99.234%' → '99.2%'). Percentages and rates need a stated basis. Cross-ref sd-014. If a needed number is unknown, write '[data needed: ...]' rather than inventing it.

title: Even a data figure needs a message title
body: A KPI/chart/table slide still earns an assertion title: not 'Q3 Metrics' but 'Yield recovered to 99.2% after the reflow fix'. The figure is the evidence for that claim. Applies to every kind — the reader should get the point from the title alone.

title: Flow vs tree vs timeline
body: Flow = a directed process with arrows (steps that transform an input), 4-6 steps. Tree = static part-of hierarchy (no time, no transformation). Timeline = events fixed in time. If steps have dates, it is a timeline; if boxes just contain sub-boxes, it is a tree; if each step feeds the next, it is a flow.

title: Compare vs table
body: Use compare (2-3 panels) when each option needs a RICH mini-figure or a short bullet list. Use table when the comparison is a grid of many attributes × options — denser and easier to scan. Two options with 5 spec rows → table. Two architectures each shown as a diagram → compare.

title: State assumptions instead of fabricating
body: When the request lacks the data a kind needs (chart values, comparison targets, timeline dates), make a reasonable qualitative draft and STATE the assumption in the caption ('가정: ...'), plus '[data needed: ...]'. Never invent precise numbers or dates to fill a template. Ask a focused question if the gap is essential.

title: Label brevity
body: Node/cell/milestone labels are scannable fragments, not sentences (≤ ~24 chars). Put the nuance in the caption or a note, not in the shape. Consistent grammatical form across peer labels (all noun phrases, or all verb phrases). Expand an acronym once, in the caption, on first use.

title: Caption carries the takeaway
body: The caption is one line stating the implication a reader should leave with — not a restatement of the axis or a label list. For a chart: 'Bandwidth roughly doubled each generation.' Keep it to a single sentence; the title makes the claim, the caption interprets the evidence.

title: One figure, one idea
body: A single figure answers one question. Do not cram a timeline plus a KPI row plus a table into one figure — pick the kind that best answers the core question and let the rest be a follow-up. If the request truly has two ideas, the standard flow is one figure per draft; suggest a second slide rather than overloading.

title: Order chart categories meaningfully
body: Order categories by a real logic: chronological for trends (line), or descending value for rankings (bar). Do not leave arbitrary/alphabetical order when a natural order exists — the ordering is itself information. Keep category labels short so the x-axis stays legible.
